title: Sets
description: HashSet<T> provides O(1) membership testing — essential for duplicate detection, set operations, and tracking visited nodes. Master insert, contains, remove, and set-theoretic operations like intersection and union.

sections.title: Creating and using HashSet
sections.explanation: Create with `HashSet::new()`. Use `.insert(value)` (returns bool — true if new, false if duplicate), `.contains(&value)` for O(1) lookup, and `.remove(&value)` to delete. HashSet is backed by a HashMap internally and requires elements to implement Hash + Eq.

exercise.prompt: Create a HashSet and insert values 1, 2, 3, 2, 1. Print the set's length (should be 3 since duplicates are ignored). Check if it contains 2 and 5, and print both results.

validation.message: Set has 3 unique elements, contains 2 but not 5
Use HashSet::new(), insert(), contains(), and len()

exercise.hint: Use .len() for size. Check contains(&5) — it was never inserted, so it returns false.

sections.title: Duplicate detection pattern
sections.explanation: A common interview pattern: iterate through a collection and use a HashSet to detect duplicates in O(n) time. If `.insert()` returns false, you've found a duplicate. This replaces the O(n log n) sorting approach when you only need to check for duplicates.

exercise.prompt: Write a function `first_duplicate` that returns the first duplicate value in a slice as Option<i32>. Test with [5, 3, 4, 2, 3, 7] (should find 3) and [1, 2, 3] (should find None).

validation.message: First duplicate in [5,3,4,2,3,7] is 3, no duplicates in [1,2,3]
Use insert's return value to detect duplicates and return Some(num) or None

exercise.hint: When insert returns false, return Some(num) — that's the duplicate.

sections.title: Set intersection, union, and difference
sections.explanation: Rust's HashSet supports mathematical set operations: `.intersection(&other)`, `.union(&other)`, `.difference(&other)`, and `.symmetric_difference(&other)`. Each returns an iterator. These are useful for problems involving common elements, merging, and exclusion.

exercise.prompt: Create two sets: a = {1,2,3,4,5} and b = {4,5,6,7,8}. Find their intersection and print it sorted. Find the union count and print it.

validation.message: Use .intersection() and .union() with collect to materialize results

exercise.hint: Call .collect() on the union iterator to materialize it into a Vec.

sections.title: Deduplicating with HashSet
sections.explanation: To remove duplicates while preserving order, iterate and insert into a HashSet as a guard. Only keep elements that successfully insert (i.e., are new). This is cleaner than sorting + dedup when order matters, and runs in O(n) time.

exercise.prompt: Complete the dedup_ordered function that removes duplicates while preserving first-occurrence order. Test with [5, 2, 8, 2, 5, 1, 8, 3].

validation.message: Use insert's bool return to guard push — only add if insert returns true

exercise.hint: seen.insert(num) returns true when the element is new — use that as the guard condition.
